2026-04-30.title: Quordle Answer Today: April 30, 2026
2026-04-30.summary: Your daily Quordle solution page for April 30, 2026. This guide walks you through today's puzzle, explains how to use this page without spoiling the answers, and tackles the most common questions players ask about Quordle's various game modes.
2026-04-30.articleHtml:
If you've landed on this page, you're probably deep into today's Quordle and looking for a little help. That's exactly what we're here for. This page gives you today's answers, explains how the game works across its different modes, and answers the questions that keep coming up in the community. Whether you need a full reveal or just a nudge in the right direction, we've got you covered.

Today's Quordle page at a glance

Today's date is April 30, 2026, which means a fresh set of Quordle puzzles is live. If you're playing Classic mode, you have the standard nine guesses to find four five-letter words simultaneously. The game presents you with four separate boards that share the same guess input, so every letter you type appears across all boards at once. Green means the letter is correct and in the right position. Yellow means the letter appears somewhere in that word but not in that spot. Gray means the letter doesn't appear in that word at all.

Beyond Classic, Quordle offers several alternative experiences. Chill mode removes the time pressure, letting you think through each guess without a clock ticking down. Extreme mode cranks up the difficulty by cutting your guesses down to six instead of nine. Sequence mode challenges you to solve the four words in a specific order, which changes your strategy significantly since you can't just knock out the easy ones first. Rescue mode gives you unlimited guesses but tracks how many attempts you need to complete all four boards. Weekly throws you a single puzzle every seven days with a special scoring system.

If you're not sure which mode suits you, Classic is the default and what most people start with. Try Chill if you find yourself rushing and making silly mistakes. Jump to Extreme once Classic feels too easy. Sequence is genuinely different enough that it's worth experiencing at least once, even if you don't make it your go-to mode.

What to pay attention to in today's Quordle puzzle

Every Quordle puzzle has its own personality based on which words the game selected. Some days you'll get four straightforward words that respond well to common starting guesses. Other days, one or more of the words will be more obscure, using letters that don't show up often in five-letter words. Today falls somewhere in between, which means a solid opening strategy will serve you well, but you'll need to stay flexible as you narrow things down.

The key thing to watch for is letter frequency. If you find yourself with three green letters in one board but you can't figure out the remaining two spots, check whether the missing letters are common or uncommon. Words with multiple vowels tend to be easier to pin down because there are only five vowels in English and most five-letter words use at least two of them. Words with rare letters like X, Z, Q, or J tend to be the trickier ones on any given day.

Pay attention to the boards that are giving you trouble. When you get a yellow or gray result on one board but green results on others, that tells you something about the letter distribution. A letter that works perfectly in one word might not appear in another at all. Don't assume a letter is absent from all four words just because it showed up gray once. The game uses each word independently, so a letter can be gray in one board and green in another.

One thing that catches players off guard is the order of elimination. You might solve three boards quickly and then hit a wall on the fourth. This often happens because you spent less mental energy on the unsolved board while focusing on the others. When you have just one board left, slow down and treat it like a fresh start. Review all your previous guesses and what you know about the letter positions you've already discovered.

How to use this page without spoiling more than you want

We get it. Sometimes you just need a hint, not a full answer. This page is designed to let you control how much help you get. If you want to figure out today's puzzle on your own but you're stuck on a specific board, you can reveal just that board's answer without seeing the others. Look for the reveal options on the puzzle page itself or use the individual board hints if they're available in the interface.

For players who want to check their answers after finishing, this page serves as your verification tool. You can compare your results against the official solutions without having to scroll through a full walkthrough. This is especially useful if you played in a mode that doesn't show you the answers immediately after winning or losing.

If you're using this page to learn strategy rather than to get today's answers, spend some time reading through the common mistakes section below. Understanding what trips people up is often more valuable than seeing the answers directly. You'll develop better instincts for letter selection and board prioritization, which makes future puzzles more enjoyable.

The archive links on this site let you revisit past puzzles. This is a fantastic study tool. You can pull up a previous day's Quordle, study the solution, and then try to understand why those particular words were chosen. Some players find it helpful to play archive puzzles in sequence to build up their vocabulary and pattern recognition. Just remember that past puzzles won't match today's board, so don't try to use old answers on today's puzzle.

Common mistakes players make on Quordle

One of the biggest mistakes is starting with the same word every single day without adjusting based on what you learn. Yes, opening with words like CRANE or SLATE is generally solid, but if you've already ruled out several letters from those common words, you might be wasting a guess on a board that won't benefit from that particular letter combination. Flexibility in your opening strategy matters more than people realize.

Another frequent error is not using the process of elimination aggressively enough. When you discover that a letter is gray on all four boards, that information is gold. It eliminates that letter from consideration for every future guess on every board. Some players get so focused on finding greens that they forget to leverage grays. A single gray result can rule out dozens of potential words if you let it.

Players also struggle with board prioritization. When you're solving four boards simultaneously, it's tempting to spread your attention evenly. But if one board is clearly further along than the others, it often makes sense to pour your mental energy into that one. Getting one board done reduces your cognitive load and lets you focus on the remaining challenges with fewer variables.

Then there's the classic mistake of second-guessing yourself mid-game. You get a result that doesn't match your mental model of the word, and instead of accepting the new information, you convince yourself that the game must be wrong or that you mis-typed something. The game is almost certainly not wrong. If you typed A and got a gray result, that letter is not in that word. Trust the feedback and adjust your thinking accordingly.

Finally, many players underestimate how much a good ending strategy matters. When you get down to your last few guesses, panic sets in and people start guessing randomly. This is the worst possible approach. Instead, take a breath, list out what you know for certain about the unsolved board, and work backward from there. Your earlier guesses have already done most of the heavy lifting. The final guesses are about confirmation, not discovery.

Questions players keep asking about Quordle

Can I play yesterday's Quordle puzzle today?

Yes, through the archive system. Every puzzle from previous days is stored and accessible. You won't find yesterday's exact puzzle on the main daily page because Quordle generates one fresh puzzle per day, but the archive lets you practice on any past puzzle you want. This is perfect for players who want extra practice or who missed a day and don't want to break their streak. The archive puzzles work exactly like the daily ones, so your practice carries over to real games.

Why does my guess show different colors on different boards?

This is one of the most common sources of confusion for new players. The color coding is calculated independently for each board. If you guess the word TRACE, the T might be green on board one because that board's word starts with T, gray on board two because that word doesn't contain T at all, and yellow on board three because that word has a T but not in the first position. The same letter can have three different meanings across four boards simultaneously. This is intentional and central to how Quordle creates its challenge.

What's the difference between Chill mode and Classic mode?

Classic mode has no time limit, so technically there's no difference in that regard. However, many players report feeling implicit pressure in Classic mode even without a timer, perhaps because of the implied challenge or the way the interface presents your guess count. Chill mode is designed to feel more relaxed, which helps some players think more clearly and make better decisions. If you've ever felt rushed even when there was no clock, try switching to Chill for a week and see if your win rate improves.

How does Sequence mode work exactly?

In Sequence mode, you must solve the four boards in a specific order that the game assigns to you. You can't see which board is which until you solve it, but you know from the start that board A must be solved before board B, and so on. This changes your strategy because you can't just knock out the easy words first. You might spend several guesses on the first board while the others sit unsolved, waiting for their turn. Sequence mode is harder than Classic mode for most players, but it's a great way to break out of comfortable habits and develop more flexible solving skills.

Is there a way to undo my last guess?

No, once you submit a guess, it's locked in. This is true across all modes. The game doesn't offer an undo function because undoing would effectively give you more than your allocated nine guesses in Classic mode. If you make a mistake, you have to work with the consequences. This is why it's worth taking an extra second before hitting enter, especially in later rounds when you have fewer guesses remaining. Some players find it helpful to type their guess in the input field and look at it for a moment before submitting, just to catch any typos or second thoughts.

Do the daily puzzles reset at midnight in my timezone?

Quordle uses a consistent reset time based on UTC. This means that depending on where you live, the daily puzzle might change at a different hour than you're expecting. For most players in North America, the reset happens in the late afternoon or evening of the previous day. For players in Asia and Australia, the reset might already have happened by the time you wake up. If you ever notice that the puzzle seems to have changed when you didn't expect it to, this timezone difference is likely the cause.

Why did my win streak reset even though I played every day?

Streaks are tied to the daily puzzle, not to calendar days. If you played yesterday's puzzle but missed the day before, your streak would break even if you played today. Some players also experience streak issues when there's a timezone confusion or when they play the puzzle just before the daily reset. The safest approach is to play early in the day in your local timezone, which gives you a buffer before the next reset. If your streak resets unexpectedly, check whether you might have missed a day or played right at the reset boundary.

Whatever help you need from this page, use it at your own pace. Quordle is meant to be fun, and there's no shame in getting a hint when you're stuck. The important thing is that you're enjoying the puzzle and maybe learning something along the way. Come back tomorrow for a fresh set of four words, and good luck with today's game.
